steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks how Text 2 responds to the criticism in Text 1. Text 2 defends Asimov by arguing his flat characters are a deliberate choice — individuals are meant to be unimportant given the sweep of history he depicts.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — By pointing out that the flatness of Asimov's characters is a feature of *The Naked Sun* and *The Stars, Like Dust* but not of *Foundation*.: Text 2 does not concede the criticism or shift the conversation away from characterization. ✗
  • Choice B — By arguing that Asimov's writing style should be judged against the styles of historians rather than those of fiction writers.: Text 2 does not compare Asimov to other authors to make its point. ✗
  • Choice C — By agreeing that Asimov's prose is flawed but disagreeing about the quality of his characters.: Text 2 does not argue that Asimov's characters are actually well-developed. ✗
  • Choice D — By asserting that Asimov's minimal characterization is likely a conscious choice that helps him convey an important idea.: This matches Text 2's argument that flat characters are a purposeful device to convey that individuals don't shape historical forces. ✓
D is correct.
